lesson.menu_desc: Reading a pushback case + first conditional -- 29 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 6 -- Handling Pushback -- Disagreeing Professionally
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Rafael Pelizaro | Aula 6 -- Handling Pushback

reading.rtitle: Handling Pushback — A Project Manager's Notes
paras: Every IT project has a moment of pushback. On the Banco Atlas project, the product owner wanted a new login feature added to the current sprint. As the project manager, Rafael did not simply say no. He knew that good disagreement is not a fight; it is a calm, professional conversation. His goal was to raise his concern, explain the constraint, and still keep a strong relationship with the stakeholder.

Rafael started by seeing the product owner's point. Then he explained his objection: the timing, not the feature itself. "If we add this feature now, we will miss the milestone on Friday," he said. There was also a trade-off with quality, because the team would not have time to test it well. He used a first conditional to make the consequence clear and reasonable, instead of just refusing the idea.

Finally, Rafael offered a compromise. If the team moved the feature to the next sprint, they would protect the deadline and still deliver it. He justified the plan with clear reasons and asked the product owner to align before the client call. The pushback did not become a conflict. Instead, both sides reached a reasonable agreement — and that is the real skill of a project manager.
reading.source: Adapted for class use from an internal IT project planning note

gist.prompt: What is the best one-line summary of this text?
choices: Rafael refused the new feature, and the product owner became angry.
Rafael disagreed professionally: he raised a concern, explained the consequence, and offered a compromise both sides accepted.
Rafael added the feature to the sprint at once to keep the product owner happy.

items: Rafael said no to the feature at once, without any explanation.
He did not simply say no; he raised a concern and explained the constraint.
Rafael's objection was the timing of the feature, not the feature itself.
He explained that his objection was the timing, not the feature itself.
Adding the feature now would have no effect on the Friday milestone.
He said that if they add it now, they will miss the milestone on Friday.
In the end, both sides reached a reasonable agreement.
They agreed on a compromise: move the feature to the next sprint.
In your own work, how do you usually react when a stakeholder pushes back on your plan?
Rafael disagreed but kept a strong relationship. Why is tone so important in a professional disagreement?
Think of a recent trade-off on your project. How did you explain it to the other side?

listenings.text: Hi, this is Rafael. I am recording a quick note about the planning call this afternoon. The product owner wants to add a new login feature to the current sprint. I understand the idea, but I have a concern about the deadline. If we add this feature now, we will miss the milestone on Friday. There is also a trade-off with quality, because the team will not have time to test it well. My objection is not the feature itself. My objection is the timing. So in the call I will see her point first, then I will explain the constraint, and then I will propose a compromise. If we move the feature to the next sprint, we will protect the deadline and still deliver it. I want us to align on the plan before we talk to the client. I will keep my tone calm and professional, and I will not just say no. I will justify my position with clear reasons and offer a reasonable option.

Hi Rafael, this is Sandra. Thank you for your note about the new feature. I see your point about the deadline, and I share your concern. You are right that if we add it now, we will probably miss Friday. I do not want to break the milestone either. Here is my assumption, and please correct me if I am wrong: the client mainly wants the feature before the end of the month, not this exact week. If that is true, your compromise works for me. Let us move the login feature to the next sprint, protect the deadline, and tell the client the new date with a clear reason. I think that is a fair and reasonable trade-off. Could you prepare a short summary of the decision, so we both align on it before the client call? If you send it today, I will review it tonight. Thank you for raising this early and so professionally.
